text: Individual Demand for Building State Effectiveness
Yetsedaw Bekele, Harry Dienes, Daniel Rogger and Giulio Schinaia ∗ Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite December, 2024

Abstract Training and professional development are essential for building an effective workforce. Yet where benefits of capacity building accrue to society, such as with public servants, individuals may under-invest in professional development relative to the social optimum. This paper presents evidence on the extent of demand for professional development in public administration. We elicit incentive-compatible willingness-to-pay for professional development opportunities among civil servants in Ethiopia. Our findings reveal prevalent low demand for professional development activities key to building state capacity. The median respondent values access to a professional development activity as much as a packet of pens. However, demand is highly elastic, and almost all public officials are willing to undertake training for a small subsidy. An ongoing follow-up experiment aims to disentangle whether the low demand is driven by low private returns to training or by externalities that respondents fail to internalize.

Keywords : bureaucrats, public administration, training JEL Classification : D73, H00, H11, H83, M53, O20 ∗ Acknowledgments: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the International Growth Centre, the World Bank’s Development Impact Evaluation i2i fund, Bureaucracy Lab and Governance Global Practice. We would like to thank Adebabay Abay, Temidayo Taiwo Falade, Devvrat Raghav, and Getnet Tesfaw for excellent research assistance, and the staff at the Ethiopian Management Institute and the Civil Service University for guidance and support. We are grateful to James Berry and Stefan Dercon, who provided very useful comments. The findings, interpretations, and conclusions expressed in this paper are entirely those of the authors. They do not necessarily represent the views of the World Bank and its affiliated organizations, or those of the Executive Directors of the World Bank or the governments they represent. Ethics has been approved by the University of Oxford with identifier ECONCIA22-23-21. PAP registration is at the AEA registry under AEARCTR-0012407. Author affiliations: Bekele - University of Messina and The World Bank; Dienes - Cornell University; Rogger - The World Bank; Schinaia - University of Chicago
Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

State capacity is a central determinant of economic development [Besley and Persson, 2009; Acemoglu, Garc ́ıa-Jimeno and Robinson, 2015; Fergusson, Molina and Robinson, 2022]. Despite its established importance, our understanding of the professional development of public servants, a key feature of strengthening state capacity, remains elusive [Grindle and Hilderbrand, 1995; Azulai et al., 2020]. Public servants are both a technical asset in themselves, transforming public policy into government action, as well as managers of other personnel and public resources. Given their extended tenures, professional development of existing public servants is a key avenue for strengthening the state and its ability to deliver. However, the social benefits of an individual public servant’s capabilities may not enter into their investment decisions regarding capacity development. Like actors in private organizations, they may under-invest in their capabilities relative to the level that optimizes organization-level objectives [Konings and Vanormelingen, 2015; Adhvaryu, Kala and Nyshadham, 2023; De Grip and Sauermann, 2012]. Unlike private actors, these under-investments have direct implications for state capacity. Together, these constraints imply that leaving the decisions for investing in state capacity to individual public servants may lead to lower state capacity than the social optimum. This paper investigates how public servant’s perceive capacity building in the public sector and measures their demand for professional development activities. To build a picture of these demands that is both precisely identified but also broadly relevant, we combine evidence from a field experiment within a particular public service context with novel survey data from an international initiative to survey public servants. To better understand the demand for public service capacity building, we designed a framed field experiment akin to a market mechanism to measure it among a sample of public sector workers in Ethiopia. This framed experiment was implemented with public servants from the Civil Service University in Addis Ababa, who are are pursuing further education in the capital and come from subnational government offices. We develop an incentive-compatible experimental measure of demand for individual capacity building among public servants by implementing a Becker-Degroot-Marshak (BDM) mechanism in
Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

the public administration. We offered the opportunity to pay (or be paid) to attend executive training sessions, professional coaching, and work shadowing. We design these professional development opportunities with input from focus groups, and they were offered by the country’s leading management institute in partnership with reputable international organizations. We find a low willingness to pay for such capacity-building opportunities. The median demand for a day’s training is equivalent to that for a packet of pens. Yet demand is also highly elastic, implying a degree of responsiveness to centralized efforts to subsidize capacity building. We find a distinctive kink in the demand curve at 0, implying lower inelasticity of demand for a subset of public officials. To understand the drivers of demand in this context, we conduct topic modelling on the free response answers given by our respondents explaining why they bid as they did. We find suggestive evidence that the more focused on individual payoffs an official is, rather than on the wider social benefits of his capacity building, the lower their willingness-to-pay. We anchor these results in survey evidence public sector administrations across 26 countries. Using new data from the Global Survey of Public Servants [Schuster et al., 2023], we describe public servant’s own perspectives across varying contexts, drawing on over 1 million individual respondents from over 1,100 different institutions. This comparative effort indicates that our results from Ethiopia are consistent with similar concerns from a broad range of settings. A large literature on workplace training in private sector settings [Blundell et al., 1999] finds that training and other professional development interventions can improve sales, profits, and management [Cusolito, Darova and McKenzie, 2023; Iacovone, Maloney and McKenzie, 2022; Martins, 2021] and improves labour market outcomes [Alfonsi et al., 2020]. Empirical work supports the view that workplace training increases productivity more than wages [Konings and Vanormelingen, 2015; Dearden, Reed and Van Reenen, 2006], including recent experimental research [De Grip and Sauermann, 2012; Adhvaryu, Kala and Nyshadham, 2023]. The tension between the relative benefits of training for employers and workers forms a key part of this literature. 1 Theoretically, Acemoglu and Pischke [1998, 1999] argue that firms 1 Recent experimental evidence from Burundi shows that employers under-invest in
Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

are able to benefit from investing in training to the extent that there are labour market imperfections that inhibit turnover and allow a compressed wage structure. Firms will invest in worker training to the extent that labour markets are not fully competitive, allowing for consequent productivity gains to be captured by the firm. Conversely, workers can expect to benefit from investing in their professional development in labour markets with fewer frictions. A nascent literature identifies positive productivity and performance impacts of training in public administration with experimental evidence [Azulai et al., 2020; Jakobsen, Jacobsen and Serritzlew, 2019; Jacobsen et al., 2022; Kroll and Moynihan, 2015] and panel data [Seidle, Fernandez and Perry, 2016], suggesting that training can have positive impacts in the public sector. However, job move transaction costs are particularly high for public sector jobs, characterized by skills that may be less transferable to similar private sector positions. This implies the incentive for workers to invest in capacity building will be particularly low. 2 There is very limited evidence on the drivers of individual demand for capacity building. This is despite the fact that the success of workplace development itself may depend on co-investment of time and effort by those undergoing development programs. 3 We are not aware of studies that meatraining of their employees because of labour market frictions and spillovers to other firms that reducing employer’s incentives [Cefala et al., 2024]. 2 Such analysis is complicated by the fact that while compression in public sector wage structures [Gindling et al., 2020] suggests that public employers will be able to capture more of the the gains from training than employees, the lack of profit signals changes the objective function of bureaucracies and their managers. Existing evidence is not clear on the the size of the wage return to training in the public sector, though an analysis of household panel data to compare the training in the public and private sectors in the USA and UK suggests large differences between the two countries [M ́endez and Sep ́ulveda, 2016]. The USA, ranked highest for bureaucratic quality, has a large return to employerprovided training in the public sector of 6 percent, against a private sector return of 2 percent. In the UK, however, the return in both sectors are smaller, and the order reverses: a 0.9 percent increase in the private sector and a 0.7 percent increase in the public sector. 3 Theoretically, training demand may be impacted by the degree employees expect their actions to lead to a corresponding wage growth, the sense that an individual’s capabilities are influential, and whether official’s believe training contributes to the achievement of professional goals. Using German household panel data, Caliendo et al. [2022] show that a higher internal locus of control leads to greater take up of training, owing to increased expectations of wage rises rather than actual wage rises. Meta-analyses of observational
Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

sure the demand for professional development or capacity building inside public administration, despite the theoretical reasons to believe individual demand will be important to the development of state capacity. As such, we contribute to the scientific understanding of public sector capacity by measuring willingness to pay in a controlled setting and embedding our findings in a comparative cross-national analysis regarding what may drive such demand structures. The paper continues as follows. Section 2 presents the results of a demand estimation exercise undertaken amongst public servants from the Government of Ethiopia. Section 3 explores the determinants of willingness-to-pay from survey data collected in parallel with the demand estimation exercise. Section 4 provides comparative evidence on the demand for training from Ethiopia and across the world. Section 5 concludes.

section_name: Capacity Building in Ethiopia’s Public Service
text: Ethiopia is Africa’s second most populous country, home to 100 million people or 10% of the population of Africa. It has a large public service, employing roughly half of paid employees in the country [Baig et al., 2021]. Corruption is less prevalent than in most of Africa, but government effectiveness is seen as relatively weak overall [Kaufmann and Kraay, 2023]. Like many public administrations, capacity building initiatives are a pillar of Ethiopia’s public service. A 2024 survey of Ethiopian public servants indicates that 43 percent had received training in the last 12 months, among whom 97 percent generally receive training at least once a year. Among respondents who attended training, 72 percent received internal training, while 23 percent went on external courses. 4 The survey indicates that the training public servants receive appears to be studies in psychology and organizational behaviour have found workplace learning is more successful when tied to clear professional goals [Kraiger and Ford, 2021] and that motivation and work climate are an important predictor of knowledge transfer from training to the workplace [Ford, Baldwin and Prasad, 2018]. 4 Further details on the surveys of public servants discussed in this section are provided in the Appendix Annex A.

section_name: Capacity Building in Ethiopia’s Public Service
text: Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

section_name: Capacity Building in Ethiopia’s Public Service
text: broadly public-sector specific. Among public servants who had received training in the previous 12 months, the most prevalent topics of study were ‘relevant laws and regulations’ (73 percent), ‘administrative processes’ (32 percent), ‘ethics’ (29 percent) and ‘core ministry subject matters’ (27 percent). While public sector organizations understandably provide their employees with public-sector specific knowledge rather than general competencies, this reduces the transferability of the training to other settings and amplifies the benefits to the wider public service. Respondents considered the training they did receive as relevant to their work (74 percent saying ‘very relevant’) and improved their workplace productivity (92 percent). These results are highly consistent across sectors, such as health, education, and finance, and across tier of government, whether federal, regional, or local. Yet the survey also pointed towards the fact that extrinsic incentives for undertaking training were also limited within the public service, despite its perceived effectiveness on productivity by public servants themselves. Only 56 percent of respondents were confident that they would be promoted if they performed well in their public service roles, reducing the incentive to invest in public-service specific capabilities. Meanwhile, continuously developing job specific skills continues to be subordinate to formal education levels, with educational background seen as very important, scoring 4.8 out of 5 on an index of what predicts promotions, whereas job-specific skills averaged 3.0, equivalent to being ‘neutral’ about the impact on promotions.

Demand To measure individual public servant demand for professional development activities within this setting, we created a market for capacity building activities in Ethiopia’s public service through a framed field experiment. We focus on three of the most frequently offered activities: 1) executive education; 2) professional coaching; and 3) work shadowing. To ensure the programs offered are of some value, we build on feedback from focus groups to offer products with valued characteristics, run by the pre-eminent management institute in the country, in partnership with international organizations, and
Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

with low costs of participation. 5 We work with public servants exploring professional development opportunities at the Civil Service University in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. Their characteristics are similar to the broader public service, as described in Table 1, where we compare their demographics to those of a representative survey of Ethiopian public servants undertaken in 2016. As in the wider public service, participants in our study are dominantly men, in their mid-thirties, who have worked in the public service for almost a decade. They typically have an undergraduate degree already, and are pursuing a further degree at the Civil Service University. Similar to the distribution of the service, they are dominantly from subnational entities, with roughly half from the lowest tier of the Federal Government—the woreda . 6 To elicit willingness to pay for professional development, we design a BeckerDeGroot-Marshak (BDM) mechanism adapted from the recent literature to our setting [Berry, Fischer and Guiteras, 2020; Cole et al., 2020; Burchardi et al., 2021]. BDM mechanisms have been used to measure willingness to pay for a wide variety of products [Berkouwer and Dean, 2022] and have begun to be used to measure demand for services such as education and training [Maffioli, McKenzie and Ubfal, 2022; Berry and Mukherjee, 2019; Abebe et al., 2024]. Our mechanism asks participants the highest amount they are willing to pay for each of three development opportunities (coaching, shadowing, training), the order of which is randomized. The bids for all three opportunities are confirmed with the subjects, with all three bids displayed simultaneously. These first bids are then tested with a series of questions that probe whether they would in fact still like to purchase the course for a small increment in price. They are also reminded that they will in fact pay for the course if the randomised price they receive turns out to be below their bid. 7 Both of the 5 Further details on the marketing of the opportunities, and the wider protocol used for the experiment, is provided in the Appendix Annex B. 6 While we cannot observe a difference in objective characteristics from our sample relative to the representative survey of the civil servants, we cannot rule out that selection at Civil Service University may be driven by other factors that we did not measure. For example, if those at Civil Service University were more motivated than the average civil servants and this motivation led them to enrol for postgraduate studies, we may expect that they may also be willing to pay more for continuous professional development. 7 While all subjects consented to participate, 13 out of 512 did not have a method of

Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

probing questions are to ensure fidelity and allow participants to carefully consider their valuation. 8 The participant is then offered a randomly selected opportunity and price. 9 If the offer is lower than the stated maximum willingness to pay for the specific course, the participant purchases the opportunity at the randomized price. 10 If the price drawn is higher, the participant cannot purchase the opportunity. This setup enables an incentive-compatible elicitation of an individualized willingness to pay for each opportunity for every participant. Since the participant does in fact buy the item for real money, but only if the randomised price is less than or equal to the bid, participants are incentivised to reveal their true valuation. The distribution of prices included negative prices, such that participants would be paid to attend the training. If subjects state a valuation of 0 Birr in their initial valuation, the respondent was then asked how much would be the minimum amount they would need to get paid in order to take part in the activity. We incorporate negative prices—akin to direct financial subsidies—in keeping with a common practice where public servants obtain per diems when attending some training, and enables us to measure demand in a context of widespread subsidies. 11 The extension to negative pricing is an important feature of our study, where public servants may not value the time spent in training as highly as their opportunity cost of time, irrespective of the social benefits of the consequent capabilities they may obtain. 12 of n = 499. One of these then stopped after the trial exchange for the pen. 8 We first ran a practice with participants to familiarise them with the mechanism by offering the chance to buy a pen using the BDM process. The willingness to pay for the pen forms a benchmark to assess the results for the development opportunities. 9 While the price for each participant was pre-randomised, it was not revealed to either the participant or the enumerator until after the bids for all courses had been made. 10 All bar four participants paid the relevant price when they were required to do so. 11 A negative price is equivalent to Willingness to Accept (WTA) in the absence of endowment effects. 12 The experiment was executed as planned, achieving high implementation fidelity. The process adhered closely to our original design, and we were able to gather the intended data effectively.
Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

Figure 1 shows the inverse demand curve for all subjects, showing the share of subjects that have a willingness to pay less than p , where p ∈ [ − 300 , 700]. The median offers for coaching, shadowing and training were 80, 90, and 100 Birr, respectively. 13 During the trial item elicitation, the median offer for a pen was 15 Birr. Equivalently, the value of the professional development opportunities was worth around 5 to 6 pens to our study participants. Relative to the median income we assess for the participants, these offers are approximately 1% of median monthly income. Only 2% of participants would pay more than 10% of monthly income for any of the development opportunities we offer.

Figure 1: Inverse Demand Curve for Coaching, Shadowing and Training 13 At the time of the study, the nominal exchange rate was 60 Ethiopian Birr (ETB) = 1 US Dollar, such that these were equivalent to USD1.40, USD1.60 and USD1.80 respectively.
Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

The inverse demand curve for training indicates that there is slightly more demand for this form of professional development than for coaching and shadowing, the inverse demand curves of which closely mirror each other. However, despite the difference in the distributions between training and both coaching and shadowing being significant in Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests ( p = 0 . 00), the substantive difference in demand between opportunities is equivalent to the value of a pen for our participants. As such, our findings seem relatively equivalent across types of capacity building. Though the demand for training is generally low, it is however very elastic— with all three inverse demand curves indicating a high degree of responsiveness to small variations in price. Small deviations in the cost—which is financial here, but is a proxy for wider effort and other costs—imply a large shift in the uptake in capacity building activities. Akin to the literature on socially desirable goods (such as insecticide treated nets) facing low demand at even small positive prices, public servants are highly price-sensitive to investing in their capabilities [Cohen and Dupas, 2010; Mobarak et al., 2012]. Such sentiments are reflected in the Ethiopian Survey of Public Servants 2024, where 82 percent of respondents expressed that there was insufficient subsidized training provided to them. Figure 1 suggests there exists a long tail of positive demand, with roughly 10% of participants willing to pay over 450 Birr for development opportunities. We can also see that the elasticity of demand falls amongst this top group, indicating that there is a small subset of public officials who are highly motivated by gaining capacity building opportunities. 14 This top 10% of respondents have on average more years of experience (10.7 years compared to 8.9 years, p = 0 . 026), higher monthly salaries (an average of 9,000 Birr compared to 7,700 Birr, p = 0 . 052), and are older (34.0 years compared to 32.2 years, p = 0 . 030)—though gender and government tier do not show significant differences. At the bottom end of the demand curve, there exists a small group of public (6%) that would not pay anything to undertake further professional development. A distinctive kink in the demand curve at a zero price reflects the salience of payment versus subsidy of capacity building in the public service environment. The elasticity of demand falls significantly at a price of zero, 14 However, none of the participants bid a price high-enough to cover their per capita training cost. As such, in a market setting no training would occur.
Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

indicating that small subsidies are almost as effective as large ones in inducing take up of capacity building. Conversely, such a drop in elasticity also implies there is a small set of public officials for which it is ‘expensive’ to induce them to take up capacity building activities, since the last few percent of officials in our data require large subsidies to initiate take up. These respondents do not exhibit particular descriptive differences to the rest of the sample, with no difference in gender, age, years experience, government tier or even wage. Seeing financial investment as a proxy for broader investments in professional development, the overall picture from our experiment is that marginal demand for professional development amongst public servants in Ethiopia is low. Though there is a small group of officials with a higher, and less elastic, demand for capacity building, even these officials make offers that are insufficient to cover the per capita cost of the capacity building initiatives.

section_name: What Influences the Demand for Capacity
text: Individual public servants are more likely to demand capacity building if they believe it will strengthen their future prospects, or if they are motivated by its impacts on the broader public good. Conversely, if they do not perceive effective returns to capacity building in their careers, or do not weigh the social benefit of these activities, they will be less willing to invest in them. To understand what drives the choices around professional development of the public servants in our study, we asked each participant to write a short reflection on what drove their pricing decisions, and the value they see in continuous professional development for their career. 15 For example, quoting from one response, “it is very important to improve my existing skill and experience for future work efficiency in my life”. Another respondent stated that, “Ethiopia needs skillful leaders in the government offices. If I get this chance I hope I will contribute to the development of the society that need my professional knowledge.” On average, respondents agreed that the training provided useful opportuni15 Given the need to drop some responses given the quality of the narrative provided, this section uses a subset of 87% of respondents used in the analysis in section 2.

text: ties for skill-building. The reflections rarely distinguished between types of capacity building. Rather, the passages are expressions of the respondent’s view of capacity building overall. To provide a further sense of the tone of the reflections, Figure 2 presents a word cloud from the responses.

section_name: What Influences the Demand for Capacity
text: In Figure 3, we explore the relationship between willingness to pay (WTP) for professional development and a set of pre-existing characteristics in our sample. Income is the only significant correlate of WTP, while other observable characteristics such as age, education level, and years of experience do not appear to influence demand. This pattern suggests that while wealthier individuals are more willing to invest in skill-building opportunities, other

section_name: What Influences the Demand for Capacity
text: Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

section_name: What Influences the Demand for Capacity
text: factors typically associated with human capital accumulation do not significantly affect demand for these types of programs. The positive correlation between WTP and income aligns with our expectations, reinforcing the notion that willingness to pay increases with financial means. 16 A key question arises: what explains the relatively low demand for professional development opportunities, especially given the widespread acknowledgment of their potential benefits? To explore this, we investigate the reasons behind the low demand, we analyzed respondents’ stated intentions behind their participation in professional development. We find that private motivations—such as career advancement—are negatively correlated with WTP. In contrast, public or collective motivations—such as contributing to social impact or benefiting the team—are positively correlated with WTP (Figure 4). The details of the analysis are in Appendix Annex C, but in summary we use an individual’s highest offer across the three capacity building initiatives as the dependant variable. We create indicator variables that capture perceptions of reasons for pursuing professional development elicited by respondents after the BDM experiment, where respondents could select more than one reason. This pattern suggests that the individuals who view the training as a means to contribute to a broader cause are more willing to pay for it than those who see it as a path to personal gain.

To deepen our understanding of demand elasticity, we plan to conduct a follow-up survey with the same sample. In this phase, respondents will be reminded of their initial valuations and informed about an upcoming round of targeted training. We will vary the emphasis placed on the type of skills offered in random order, asking respondents to reassess their WTP based on four different framings: • General skills for careers in the (public or private) sector • Skills that enhance social impact • Skills that benefit the respondent’s team or organization 16 Appendix Annex D also shows that while individuals reported WTP across activities is positive and above 0.6, there is still variation within individual, such that respondents did not just report the same valuation for each activity.
Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

Additionally, respondents were exposed to an information recording that emphasized different scopes of the training. By comparing WTP across these different framings, we aimed to assess whether varying the perceived benefits—either private or public—would influence demand.
Figure 3: Relationship between individual characteristics and willingness-to-pay

section_name: Individual Incentives for Building the Ca-
text: To embed the results of our field experiment into a broader view of public sector capacity building, we draw on surveys of public servants from across a range of other countries. The Global Survey of Public Servants brings together large-scale, harmonized, and comparable survey data on public servants from around the world [Schuster et al., 2023]. The survey measures employee attitudes and experience of management practices applicable to

section_name: Individual Incentives for Building the Ca-
text: Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

section_name: Individual Incentives for Building the Ca-
text: Figure 4: Relationship between reasons for capacity building and willingness-to-pay

section_name: Individual Incentives for Building the Ca-
text: capacity building. 17 As such, it provides a means of assessing whether the dynamics identified in the Ethiopian setting are prevalent in public administrations elsewhere. While we do not have willingness-to-pay estimates across countries, we find many of the institutional features elaborated above in the comparative data. Training is a pillar of public service life. On average, over 54 percent of public servants reported attending a training program in the previous two years. Yet demand is high at low prices, consistent with our results. A large proportion of public servants globally do not feel there are adequate subsidized training opportunities. Figure 5 shows the distribution from the countries where the surveys asked about whether subsidized training provision is sufficient. On average, only 60 percent of officials interviewed agree that training offerings at low prices are sufficient, while just 17 percent of surveyed institutions across 17 Further details on the data used in this section is available at the Global Survey of Public Servants web site: www.globalsurveyofpublicservants.org.

section_name: Individual Incentives for Building the Ca-
text: Note: Sourced from the Global Survey of Public Servants. Average percent of respondents agreeing that training is sufficient are shown at the national level (red) and organization level (blue) by country. Figure 5: Perception of adequate training (Institution level)

section_name: Individual Incentives for Building the Ca-
text: Indeed, the evidence shows that professional development is unequally distributed. We see substantial variation by organization in training rates. Romania, for example, has organizations with 1 percent of respondents attending training, and other organizations with 89 percent attending training. Lithuania, with the highest national average, still shows institutions varying between 45 percent and 94 percent. Such stark variations pose an important question regarding the efficiency of existing professional development activities. The global survey evidence suggests civil servants are effectively recognising training needs, highlighting that the uneven allocation of professional development may be hampering state capabilities. The global survey data show a positive correlation in the level of public servant perceptions of having the

section_name: Individual Incentives for Building the Ca-
text: Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite

section_name: Individual Incentives for Building the Ca-
text: skills they need for their job, and perceptions that training is sufficient. In four countries with comparable data, there is a strong ( ρ = 0 . 489) and highly significant ( p < 0 . 000) correlation between perceptions of skills and adequate training (where we account for country-level fixed effects): civil servants in institutions with low perceived skill adequacy are also saying training is insufficient, suggesting that adequate training is a foundation for a high skilled public service.

This paper investigates the demand for public service capacity building activities amongst public servants. We implement a Becker-DeGroot-Marshak mechanism adapted from the recent literature to our setting. We find low latent demand for such activities, with ‘training’ slightly more valued than coaching or work shadowing. The demand curve is, however, highly elastic, and at low levels of individual disutility—a low cost of access and limited personal financial burden—the vast majority of public servants will take up capacity building opportunities. Our study contributes to the growing literature on demand for continuous professional development, complementing existing evidence from the private sector [Maffioli, McKenzie and Ubfal, 2022; Abebe et al., 2024]. Descriptive evidence indicates that the individual demand is higher when individuals report public or collective benefits. Future planned research aims to disentangle whether the low demand is driven by low private returns to training or by externalities that respondents fail to internalize (see Appendix Section E). Understanding this distinction is crucial for designing policies that can more effectively stimulate demand for skill-building in the public sector.
Preliminary and incomplete, please do not cite
